summary: Korean-American founder of video art and member of the Fluxus movement. Known for installations using CRT televisions and video cameras. Collaborated frequently with cellist Charlotte Moorman on TV Cello, TV Bra, and Opera Sextronique. Major works include Electronic Superhighway (map of the US from 336 TVs and neon, at the Smithsonian), TV Buddha, Good Morning Mr. Orwell (1984 satellite broadcast seen by 25 million), and Danger Music pieces. Also created TV Fish, TV Garden, TV Clock, and Global Groove. Coined the term 'electronic superhighway' in a 1974 report.

works.name: Electronic Superhighway: Continental U.S., Alaska, Hawaii (1995)
works.description: A large-scale video installation depicting a map of the United States made from approximately 300 CRT television screens and neon tubing. Scenes from The Wizard of Oz play on the Kansas section. Alaska and Hawaii are depicted with neon lights. Currently at the Smithsonian American Art Museum. Wikipedia

works.name: Good Morning, Mr. Orwell (1984)
works.description: A live satellite broadcast / video installation aired on New Year's Day, January 1, 1984 — in ironic response to Orwell's dystopian year. Hosted by journalist George Plimpton. Featured Merce Cunningham dancing with time-delayed images of himself, John Cage improvising, Thompson Twins performing 'Hold Me Now', and Laurie Anderson and Peter Gabriel performing the title song. Broadcast from Centre Pompidou in Paris and WNET in New York; a satellite link disruption marred a planned 'space yodel.' Wikipedia

works.name: Danger Music (Playable Pieces)
works.description: A series of Fluxus-style 'Danger Music' and 'Playable Pieces' — performance art pieces with extreme or absurdist instructions. One instructs the performer to 'crawl into the womb of a living whale'; another ('Danger Music for Dick Higgins') has the same instruction. One 'Playable Piece' instructs the performer to 'cut your left forearm a distance of ten centimeters,' which was performed by composer Joseph Byrd. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Danger Music piece instructing the performer to 'crawl into the womb of a living whale'
examples: A piece of 'danger music' by this artist instructs the player to 'climb inside the vagina of a live female whale.'

examples: This artist proposed that an 'acoustic space' could be created if you put 'all the politicians from the world ... inside a circle' and activated '23 machine guns at the same time.'

comprehensive_summary:
Nam June Paik is identified in quizbowl as the Korean-American founder and father of video art, and a prominent member of the Fluxus avant-garde movement. His work centered on the idea that televisions would link the world together, and he was among the first artists to use CRT television sets and video cameras as artistic media.

His most-clued work is Electronic Superhighway: Continental U.S., Alaska, Hawaii, a sprawling map of the United States constructed from approximately 336 television screens, neon tubing, and some 3,750 feet of cable, currently housed at the Smithsonian American Art Museum. The Kansas segment notably plays scenes from The Wizard of Oz, and each state shows a unique video feed. Neon lights and light bulbs are placed over the televisions to represent state borders. The installation's title derives from a 1974 report Paik wrote called 'Media Planning for the Postindustrial Society,' in which he coined the phrase 'electronic superhighway' and envisioned a future broadband communication network incorporating 'audio cassettes, telex, data pooling' and other technologies — a vision he set '26 years from now.'

TV Buddha (1974) is another signature piece: a Buddha statue placed before a closed-circuit television that records and displays its own live image, so that the statue appears to watch itself. Paik created numerous depictions of the Buddha observing itself through closed-circuit television systems. He also created installations called Portable God that explored Buddhist themes and religious iconography.

Paik's most famous collaborative works involve cellist Charlotte Moorman, whom he described as his assistant. He constructed TV Cello — a playable instrument made from stacked television sets — for Moorman to perform on. He also designed the TV Bra for Living Sculpture, a brassiere whose cups are small TV screens, worn by Moorman during performances. Moorman was arrested during the 1967 performance Opera Sextronique for performing in various states of undress. The TV Cello was created in the aftermath of that arrest.

Good Morning, Mr. Orwell was a live satellite broadcast aired on New Year's Day, January 1, 1984 — a deliberate ironic response to Orwell's dystopian year. Watched by 25 million people, it was broadcast from the Centre Pompidou in Paris and WNET in New York via satellite. The event was hosted by journalist George Plimpton and featured Merce Cunningham dancing with time-delayed images of himself, John Cage and Merce Cunningham improvising over a Salvador Dali poem recording, Thompson Twins performing 'Hold Me Now,' and Laurie Anderson and Peter Gabriel performing 'Excellent Birds.' The broadcast opens with a pair of lips repeating its title phrase in English and French as pixelated lightning bolts strike. Problems with a satellite link to France disrupted a 'space yodel' Paik had planned. A follow-up broadcast titled 'Bye Bye Kipling' alluded to 'never shall the twain meet.'

As a Fluxus member, Paik created a series of 'Danger Music' pieces and 'Playable Pieces' with extreme instructions. His Danger Music for Dick Higgins instructs the performer to 'crawl into the womb of a living whale.' Another Playable Piece instructs the performer to 'cut your left forearm a distance of ten centimeters' — this was performed by composer Joseph Byrd. He also proposed that an 'acoustic space' could be created by placing 'all the politicians from the world inside a circle' and activating '23 machine guns at the same time.'

Biographically, Paik is noted for having once leapt off a stage to cut the necktie of composer John Cage, also pouring shampoo over him during a performance. Channeling Cage's prepared pianos, Paik created 'prepared' televisions by altering the voltages of 13 TVs and using magnets to distort their images. In a 1962 performance, he played the Moonlight Sonata while slowly doing a striptease. His breakthrough came when he used a newly purchased Sony Portapak to film Pope Paul VI's arrival in New York City. His wife was artist Shigeko Kubota, who created documentary and sculptural homages to Marcel Duchamp (including the Duchampiana series with TVs embedded in a staircase) and who performed 'Vagina Painting.' Other notable works include TV Fish (TVs placed in front of fish tanks, 1975), TV Garden (TVs among tropical plants, using footage from Global Groove), TV Clock (24 TVs on pedestals arranged in an arc, 1963), The More, The Better (a conical tower of televisions), V-yramid, Media Shuttle, and distorted video for Merce Cunningham's Variations V. He also used a video synthesizer to distort sections of a Nicholas Ray film. Amanda Kim's 2023 documentary about Paik is titled 'The Moon is the Oldest TV.' A Guggenheim retrospective featured his work with Charles Falco as technical advisor.
